behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watcher behavior (recursive/child-path event delivery), which is OS-dependent. MOCK-MISS/MOCK-HIDE: mocked watcher events can falsely pass even if real OS notifications for subdirectories are not wired correctly. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is only about file-watch processing ("file placed inside a subdirectory ... is processed"), with no AI/ML output dependency (so not Agentic) and no multi-agentic sequence (so not Workflow).
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree on the host OS, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that nested path, and assert exactly one transformed output appears in the output directory for that nested file.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior targets path+time-window decision logic and does not require OS/network/subprocess. A single dedup component with injected clock can expose real failures in window comparison and keying. Rejected higher levels: the behavior text is explicitly "deduplication logic ... same file path within a configurable time window," which disqualifies System (no real infra required), Agentic (no model output), and Workflow (no multi-step agent pipeline).
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate dedup logic with a fake/injected clock, submit the same file path twice within the configured window and assert second request is dropped, then advance clock past window and assert next request is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on real OS event duplication semantics ("operating system emits multiple filesystem events"). In-process fakes can miss debounce/coalescing bugs that appear only with actual watcher backends. Rejected higher levels: text concerns filesystem events and single processing outcome only; no AI/ML correctness dependency and no multi-agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events on the target OS (e.g., write+rename/flush), capture processing invocations/output artifacts, and assert exactly one processing result for that file path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is OS-level watcher lifecycle behavior (directory delete/recreate while process stays alive). MOCK-HIDE: mocked watcher streams rarely reproduce inode/handle invalidation and reattach edge cases. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is infrastructure resilience only, not AI/ML output quality and not multi-agentic sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, delete that directory during runtime, recreate it at the same path, then write a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real file metadata and concurrent arrival timing, which can diverge from mocked/in-memory assumptions (timestamp resolution, event ordering). To catch real failures, use real FS stats/events. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is deterministic timestamp-based ordering, with no model judgment and no multi-agentic pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: In a real watched directory, create several files in rapid succession/concurrently, record each file's creation timestamp as observed by the daemon's stat path, then assert processing order matches the daemon's documented timestamp ordering rule (including tie-break policy if timestamps are equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at real FS/write-event boundary: partial-write visibility, event timing, and completion detection are OS/filesystem dependent. Mocked writes can hide truncated-read races. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is file readiness gating only, with no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agentic sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real directory, begin writing a large file in chunks while keeping the writer open, trigger arrival event naturally, assert daemon does not process during active writes, then close writer and assert processing starts and output reflects complete file content.
